docs.title: Git EscapeRoom Labs
docs.text:
Decryption Tip

If the content doesn’t appear after entering the password in the selected room, please refresh the page (F5 or Ctrl+R).

docs.title: 🔀 Git Escape Room
docs.text: Learn Git from Zero to Hero through 50 Interactive Puzzle Rooms

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• There are several ways to run the Git-EscapeRoom Labs.
  • Choose the method that works best for you.
🐳 Docker (Recommended) Killercoda📜 From Source Using Google Cloud Shell

Using Docker is the easiest way to get started locally with the labs:

# GitHub Container Registry
docker run -it --pull=always -p 3000:3000 ghcr.io/nirgeier/git-escaperoom

Prerequisites:

  • Docker and Docker Compose installed on your system
  • No additional setup required
  • The easiest way to get started with the labs
  • Learn in your browser without any local installation

🌐 Launch on Killercoda

Benefits:

  • No installation required
  • Pre-configured environment
  • Works on any device with a web browser
  • All tools pre-installed

For those who prefer to run it directly on their machine:

# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/nirgeier/Git-EscapeRoom
# Change to the Labs directory
cd Git-EscapeRoom/Labs
# Start with the setup lab
cd assets
# Follow the instructions in the README of each lab
cat README.md

Prerequisites:

  • A Unix-like operating system (Linux, macOS, or Windows with WSL)
  • Basic command-line tools
  • Google Cloud Shell provides a free, browser-based environment with all necessary tools pre-installed.
  • Click on the Open in Google Cloud Shell button below:

  • The repository will automatically be cloned into a free Cloud instance.
  • Use CTRL + click to open it in a new window.
  • Follow the instructions in the README of each lab.

Benefits:

  • No local installation required
  • Pre-configured environment
  • Works on any device with a web browser
  • All tools pre-installed
  • Free tier available

docs.title: Intro
docs.text:
• This tutorial teaches Git through puzzles designed as escape rooms.
  • Each room covers a different Git concept - from basic installation to plumbing commands and internals.
  • Every room folder includes a README that describes the room’s objectives, hints, rules, and how to verify the solution.
  • The Git Escape Room is a series of Git puzzles (Escape-rooms, similar to the real-life attractions).
  • The puzzles are designed to teach players Git skills & features from beginner to expert level.

docs.title: Pre-Requirements
docs.text:
• This tutorial will test your Git and Linux skills.
  • You should be familiar with the following topics:
  • Basic Linux commands and shell navigation
  • Basic knowledge of a text editor (vim, nano, etc.)
  • Basic knowledge of Docker (if you choose to run it with Docker)

docs.title: Room Links
docs.text: # Room Summary 01 Install & Verify Git Check if Git is installed, explore its binary location and sub-commands 02 Git Configuration Configure Git identity, editor, and preferences with git config 03 Git Init Initialize a repository and explore the .git directory structure 04 The Three States of Git Understand Git’s three areas: Working Directory, Staging Area, and Repository 05 Git Add: Staging Deep Dive Master git add with patterns, patches, and interactive staging 06 Git Status & Restore Read git status output and use git restore to manage file states 07 Git Commit: Saving Snapshots Create commits, write good messages, and use git commit options 08 Git Log: Exploring History Navigate commit history with git log formats, filters, and search 09 Git Diff: Comparing Changes Compare changes with git diff between working dir, staging, and commits 10 Git Branch: Parallel Universes Create, switch, list, and manage branches with git branch and git switch 11 Git Merge: Bringing Branches Together Combine branches with git merge, fast-forward and three-way merges 12 Merge Conflicts Resolve merge conflicts by editing conflict markers and completing the merge 13 Git Rebase: Linear History Rewrite history with git rebase and interactive rebase for clean timelines 14 Git Stash: Emergency Shelf Save and restore work-in-progress with git stash 15 Git Remote: Connecting to the World Manage remote repositories with git remote - add, rename, remove 16 Git Clone: Copying Repositories Clone repositories with depth, branch, and bare options 17 Git Fetch: Download Without Merging Download remote changes without merging using git fetch 18 Git Pull: Fetch + Merge Fetch and integrate remote changes with git pull and pull strategies 19 Git Push: Sharing Your Work Share commits with git push, set upstream, and manage remote branches 20 Git Tag: Marking Milestones Mark releases and milestones with lightweight and annotated tags 21 Git Cherry-Pick: Selective Commits Pick specific commits from other branches and apply them 22 Git Reset: Rewinding History Rewind history with git reset - soft, mixed, and hard modes 23 Git Revert: Safe Undo Safely undo commits by creating a new reverting commit 24 Git Commit –Amend: Fixing Mistakes Fix the last commit - change message, add files, or update content 25 Git Blame: Line-by-Line Detective Track down who changed what and when - line-by-line attribution 26 Git Bisect: Bug Hunting Binary search through history to find the commit that introduced a bug 27 Git Reflog: Your Safety Net Recover lost commits, branches, and undone work from the reflog 28 Git Alias: Custom Shortcuts Create shortcuts for your most-used Git commands 29 Gitignore: Hiding Files from Git Keep unwanted files out of your repo with .gitignore patterns 30 Gitattributes: File-Level Rules Control line endings, diff behavior, and merge strategies 31 Git Hooks: Automation Triggers Automate workflows with scripts that run at key Git lifecycle points 32 Git Submodule: Repos Within Repos Embed external repositories inside your project as submodules 33 Git Worktree: Parallel Branches Work on multiple branches simultaneously without stashing 34 Git Sparse-Checkout: Partial Repos Clone only the parts of a repo you need 35 Git Assume-Unchanged: Hiding Local Edits Tell Git to ignore local changes to tracked files 36 Git Clean: Remove Untracked Files Remove untracked files and directories from your working tree 37 Git Archive: Export Without .git Export a snapshot of your repository without the .git directory 38 Git Bundle: Offline Transfer Transfer repositories offline using Git bundles 39 Git Format-Patch & Am: Email Patches Create and apply patches for email-based collaboration 40 Git Grep: Search Tracked Files Search through tracked files and commit history 41 Git Shortlog: Contributor Stats Summarize commit activity by contributor 42 Git Describe: Human-Readable Names Get human-readable names for commits based on nearby tags 43 Git Notes: Commit Metadata Attach extra metadata to commits without changing their hashes 44 Git Replace: Transparent Object Swap Transparently swap Git objects to rewrite history 45 Git Filter-Branch & Filter-Repo Rewrite entire repository history - the nuclear option 46 Git Rerere: Reuse Recorded Resolution Let Git remember and reuse conflict resolutions 47 Git Fsck: Filesystem Check Check the integrity of your Git repository 48 Git GC: Garbage Collection Optimize your repository by cleaning up and compressing objects 49 Git Plumbing Commands: Internals Peek under the hood with Git’s low-level plumbing commands 50 Git Object Model: The Four Objects Understand the four fundamental Git objects that power everything 99 The Final Exam The grand escape - prove your Git mastery!

docs.title: Room 01 - Installing Git
docs.text:
• Git is a distributed version control system created by Linus Torvalds in 2005.
  • Before you can use Git, you need to make sure it’s installed and working.

📜 Your mission

Your first mission: Confirm Git is installed and discover its secrets.

  1. Run: git --version Verify that git is installed

  2. Run: which git This shows you where git in called from.

  3. Find the password:

    • Look inside the “room01” git repository in this room.
    • The password is hidden in the commit history.
    • Hint: use git log to reveal it.

Decrypt the next room: next <PASSWORD>

docs.title: Key Commands
docs.text: Command Purpose git --version Show installed Git version which git Show path to git binary git --exec-path Show Git’s libexec directory git help List common Git commands git help -a List ALL available Git commands git help <command> Show help for specific command man git Manual page for Git

docs.title: Installing Git on Different Systems
docs.text:
# macOS (Homebrew)
brew install git

# Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install git

# Fedora/RHEL
sudo dnf install git

# Arch
sudo pacman -S git

# Alpine (this container)
apk add git

# Windows
# Download from https://git-scm.com/downloads
# Or use: winget install Git.Git

# Verify installation
git --version

docs.title: How Git Differs From Other VCS
docs.text: mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Centralized["Centralized VCS - SVN"] S["🖥️ Server"] --> D1["Dev1 (working copy)"] S --> D2["Dev2 (working copy)"] S --> D3["Dev3 (working copy)"] end subgraph Distributed["Distributed VCS - Git"] R["🌐 Remote A"] <--> G1["Dev1 (full repo)"] R <--> G2["Dev2 (full repo)"] R <--> G3["Dev3 (full repo)"] G1 <--> G2 G2 <--> G3 end

docs.title: 01. Verify Git Is Installed
docs.text:
The very first thing to check on any machine - is Git actually available?

Hint: git --version

Solution 
git --version
# Expected output: git version 2.x.x
# If you see "command not found", Git is not installed.

docs.title: 02. Locate the Git Binary
docs.text:
Find where the git executable lives on this system.

Hint: which git, type git

Solution 
which git
# /usr/bin/git  (typical Linux/Alpine)

# Alternative:
type git
# git is /usr/bin/git

docs.title: 03. Explore Git’s Built-in Help
docs.text:
Git ships with built-in documentation. List the most common Git commands.

Hint: git help, git help -a

Solution 
# Show the most common commands grouped by category
git help

# List ALL installed Git sub-commands (there are many!)
git help -a

# Get help for a specific command
git help init
git help config

docs.title: 04. Find the Git Libexec Directory
docs.text:
Git stores helper programs in a special libexec directory. Find its path.

Hint: git --exec-path

Solution 
git --exec-path
# Example: /usr/libexec/git-core

# List what's inside
ls $(git --exec-path) | head -20
# You'll see git-add, git-commit, git-log, etc.

docs.title: 05. Explore the room01 Repo
docs.text:
This room contains a room01 git repository. Enter it and look around.

Hint: cd room01, ls -la, git status

Solution 
cd room01
ls -la
# You should see files and a .git/ directory

git status
# Shows the current state of the working directory

docs.title: 06. Read the Commit History
docs.text:
The password is hidden in the commit messages of the room01 repo. Use the log to find it.

Hint: git log, git log --oneline

Solution 
cd room01
git log
# Read through the commit messages - one of them contains the password

# Compact view:
git log --oneline
# Each line shows: <short-hash> <commit-message>

docs.title: 07. Show a Specific Commit’s Details
docs.text:
Once you spot an interesting commit, inspect its full details.

Hint: git show <hash>

Solution 
# Show the latest commit
git show HEAD

# Show a specific commit by hash
git show abc1234

# This displays the commit metadata AND the diff

🔓 Unlock Room 02

Once you have the password:

next <PASSWORD>
